---
title: Digital Tools
metadata:
  teaching: 40
  exercises: 40
---

## Module Overview

- **Module:** EDS-M1 -  Digital Tools 
- **Course:** Essential Digital Skills (EDS)
- **Audience:** Early Career Researchers (PhD, Postdoc)  
- **Duration:** ~40 minutes directed teaching (+4 exercises)  

---

::: callout-outcomes

## 💡 Learning Outcomes

- Navigate core UoN digital ecosystem
- Apply digital best practices in collaboration & file organisation
- Manage your research identity
- Identify essential practices for secure research
:::

---

::: callout-questions

## ❓ Questions

1. What basic data tools I need to do research at UoN?
2. How to I maintain a consistent online researcher identity?
3. What digital tools may I need to support my research aims?  
:::

## Structure & Agenda

1. Introduction to the Digital Skills Programme (~10+10 min)  
2. Institutional Systems for Digital Research (~10+10 min)  
3. Research Identity and Profiles (~10+10 min)  
4. Digital Security & Good Practice (~10+10min)  

> 🔧 4 Activities spaced every 10 minutes.

# Introduction to the Digital Skills Programme

![](fig/office365.png){fig-align="center" width=500px}

## What is DS?

The **Digital Skills** programme equips researchers with the competencies required for modern, data-driven scholarship. it focuses on four interconnected themes:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["Digital Collaboration"]:::a --> B["Data Management"]:::b
    B --> C["Coding"]:::c --> D["Reproducibility"]:::d --> A
    classDef a fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:1px
    classDef b fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px
    classDef c fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef d fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px
```

> 💡 *Digital collaboration, data management, coding, and reproducibility form the foundation of digital research practice.*


## Beyond Tools

The programme is not simply about tools:

It is about **connecting researchers** with the people who work with data, including **data stewards**, **software engineers**, and **information specialists**. These collaborations enable better use of data, improve the quality of research outputs, and support a more open and reproducible research culture.

> 🤝 *Digital skills grow through collaboration between researchers and data professionals.*

---

## Connecting Researchers and Data Professionals


Modern research relies on shared understanding between disciplinary experts and technical specialists.

| **Benefit** | **Description** |
|--------------|-----------------|
| **Quality** | Consistent data standards and metadata practices |
| **Reproducibility** | Shared methods and transparent workflows |
| **Efficiency** | Collaborative problem-solving across domains |
| **Insight** | Combining technical and disciplinary perspectives |

> 💬 *Bridging communities builds stronger, more reproducible research.*


## Why Digital Skills Matter

Digital skills enable collaboration and efficiency across the research lifecycle.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    A["Data Collection"] --> B["Analysis"] --> C["Collaboration"] --> D["Publication & Sharing"]
    D --> A
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px
```

> 🔁 *Digital literacy underpins the entire cycle of modern research.*

## Addressing Common Challenges

This course is about addressing the following challanges at a unversity level. 

| Challenge | Digital Skills Response |
|------------|-------------------------|
| Disconnected research silos | Shared tools and workflows (Teams, GitHub, RIS) |
| Data mismanagement | Version control, structured storage, FAIR principles |
| Limited collaboration | Integrated communication and documentation tools |
| Poor reproducibility | Use of open standards, coding literacy, and transparent sharing |

> ⚙️ *Digital skills convert research challenges into structured improvements.*

## Collaboration Leads to Better Research

Collaboration is at the heart of the DS&DSP. By connecting researchers, data professionals, and support specialists, the programme builds a **community of digital practice** that strengthens the quality, openness, and impact of research across disciplines.

> “Collaboration is not an add-on—it is the mechanism through which good research becomes great research.”

## Course Structure

The programme offers **three tiers of learning**, from foundational to applied and specialist skills.

| **Level** | **Focus** | **Example Modules** |
|------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Foundation** | Core digital research competencies | Essential Digital Skills |
| **Intermediate** | Theory and collaboration in data science | Python, R, Software Engineering |
| **Advanced** | Applied analysis and discipline-specific practice | Bioinformatics, HPC, ML/AI |

> 🧩 *Each tier builds practical capability and supports research independence.*

### Esential Digital Skills

| Course                             | Description                                                                 | Duration       | Note |
|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|------|
| Essential Digital Skills – Foundation | Introduces the tools, practices, and principles needed to participate confidently in digital research. | 4× half days   |      |
| Methods for Data Science – Theoretical    | Introduces coding, software, and collaboration practices needed for sustainable research. | 4× half days   |      |
| Methods for Data Science – Applied Python | Provides practical experience in data analysis and interpretation using Python. | 2 days         | Introductory and intermediate |
| Methods for Data Science – Applied R      | Provides practical experience in data analysis and interpretation using R.     | 2 days         | Introductory and intermediate |
| Advanced       | —           | 1 day    |      |

---

::: callout-task

#### Task 1: Digital Skills Reflection

Let’s start by reflecting on your current digital research skills.  

❓ *What’s digital skills or tool you want to develop this year?*  

:::

# Institutional Systems for Digital Research

![](fig/sharepoint.png){fig-align="center" width=500px}

## Why Institutional Systems Matter

Digital research is inherently collaborative. Modern scholarship relies not only on individual skill but also on the ability to connect people, data, and systems across institutional and disciplinary boundaries.  

---

At the University of Nottingham (UoN), researchers operate within a digital ecosystem designed to **enable collaboration, safeguard data, and streamline research workflows**.  

---

Institutional systems such as **Microsoft 365**, **UniCore**, and the **Research Information System (RIS)** form the backbone of this ecosystem. Together, they support communication, data sharing, project administration, and the formal recording of research outputs and impact.

---

These platforms are more than administrative tools—they are **enablers of collaborative, transparent, and efficient digital research**.

---

## Microsoft 365: A Collaboration Platform for Research

The Microsoft 365 suite provides an integrated environment for communication, writing, data management, and project coordination.  
Its tools are foundational to digital collaboration at UoN and support nearly every aspect of day-to-day research activity.

---

### Core Tools

- **Word & Excel:** writing, data capture, and collaborative editing  
- **OneDrive:** secure personal cloud storage with autosave and version control  
- **Teams:** shared digital workspaces for communication, meetings, and group file management  
- **SharePoint:** institutional platform for structured, long-term file sharing and documentation  

---

## Collaboration in Practice

Microsoft 365 enables **seamless real-time collaboration** between researchers, administrators, and external partners:

- Multiple users can co-edit the same file simultaneously with full **version tracking**.  
- Integrated **chat and meeting tools** in Teams facilitate contextual discussion—reducing reliance on fragmented email threads.  
- Shared **document libraries** on SharePoint ensure continuity and access control for ongoing research projects.  
- **OneDrive synchronisation** allows researchers to work offline, with changes automatically updated across devices when reconnected.  

---

### File Collaboration Best Practices

- Use **Teams or SharePoint** for group projects rather than emailing attachments.  
- **Enable autosave** in Word and Excel to prevent data loss.  
- Use **version history** to restore previous document states—avoid renaming files manually.  
- Plan clear, logical **folder structures** reflecting project phases or work packages.  
- Apply **access permissions** carefully, ensuring collaborators have appropriate visibility and editing rights.

---

> 💡 *Good digital housekeeping supports not only collaboration, but also compliance, reproducibility, and research continuity.*

---

## UoN Tools: UniCore & RIS

| Tool     | Purpose                                     |
|----------|---------------------------------------------|
| **UniCore**  | Finance, HR, and procurement management     |
| **RIS**      | Research lifecycle, funding, and outputs management |

These platforms connect the practical and administrative sides of research, ensuring transparency, traceability, and efficiency across departments and projects.

---

### UniCore Quick Facts

**UniCore** is UoN’s enterprise resource system (Oracle-based) for managing financial and HR operations.

---

It is used for:

- Equipment purchases and procurement  
- Expense claims and travel reimbursement  
- Leave management and sickness reporting  
- HR administration and employment records  

> 🧭 *Think of UniCore as the infrastructure that supports the financial and operational side of your research.*

---

### RIS (Research Information System)

**RIS** (based on Worktribe) is the system through which research projects are proposed, recorded, and tracked across their lifecycle.  
It is the **single authoritative source** for research-related activity at UoN and underpins compliance, reporting, and REF submission.

---

RIS is used for:

- Grant applications and costings  
- Research outputs and publication tracking  
- Impact recording and open access compliance  
- Repository integration and ORCID synchronisation  

---

RIS is central to managing the *institutional memory* of research: it ensures that outputs, collaborations, and impact are captured systematically and linked to individuals, funders, and departments.

---

## Integrating Systems for Research Excellence

The power of these systems lies in their **interoperability**.  
RIS, SharePoint, and Microsoft 365 together form a digital infrastructure that connects research administration with active collaboration and dissemination.

---

| Research Task | Recommended System | Collaboration Benefit |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Co-author a paper | OneDrive / Word | Real-time editing, version control |
| Submit a funding bid | RIS | Shared proposal development and compliance |
| Claim travel expenses | UniCore | Secure and traceable process |
| Share project datasets | Teams / SharePoint | Controlled access and long-term storage |
| Manage project milestones | Teams | Integrated communication and documentation |
| Record outputs and impact | RIS + ORCID | Automatic linking across systems |

---

## The Broader Vision: Connected Digital Research

By using institutional systems effectively:

- Teams build **trust and accountability** through transparent processes.  
- Data is **managed and shared responsibly**, in line with open research principles.  
- Projects become **more resilient**, as institutional systems ensure continuity even when personnel change.  
- Researchers can focus on **innovation and discovery**, not file management or administration.

> “Digital collaboration is the infrastructure of modern research.  
> Tools like Teams, SharePoint, and RIS aren’t just platforms—they are the connective tissue that holds interdisciplinary research together.”

---

::: callout-task

#### Task 2: Match Research Tasks to Tools

::: panel-tabset
##### Activity

In groups of 4-5, discuss and list the digital tools you would use for each of the following:

1. Co-write a manuscript  
2. Submit a funding bid  
3. Order equipment  
4. Share large datasets with your team  

##### Reflection

- Share your answers with the plenary  
- Reflect on whether you've used any of these systems before  
- Identify any gaps in your familiarity that you want to address  

##### Answers

- **Microsoft Word -> Sharepoint + Teams** are ideal for collaborative document editing  
- **RIS (Worktribe)** is used for grant applications and research output tracking  
- **UniCore** handles finance, procurement, HR and expenses  
- **Microsoft Teams + SharePoint** is best for long-term group file access and collaboration  
:::
:::

# Research Identity & Profiles

![](fig/orchid.png){fig-align="center" width=500px}

## Why Research Identity Matters

In an increasingly digital and collaborative research environment, your **research identity** is the foundation of your professional visibility, credibility, and impact. 

---

Establishing and maintaining a coherent digital identity ensures that your work is **discoverable**, **attributed correctly**, and **linked** across the research ecosystem—from institutional systems to international repositories.

---

A consistent identity allows your publications, data, and contributions to be recognised automatically across journals, funders, and digital infrastructure. It also makes collaboration more efficient by helping others to find, verify, and engage with your work.

> 🧩 *A strong research identity connects your professional presence across platforms and ensures lasting recognition of your contributions.*

---

## ORCID – A Persistent Digital Identifier

**ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID)** is the international standard for uniquely identifying researchers.  
It provides a **persistent, 16-digit identifier** that travels with you across your career, regardless of changes in institution, discipline, or name.

---

### Why ORCID is Essential

- **Universal interoperability:** recognised by funders, publishers, and research systems worldwide  
- **Automatic updates:** publications and outputs can be added automatically via Crossref and DataCite  
- **Long-term portability:** remains active even after you move institutions or change roles  

---

Creating and maintaining an ORCID profile is a foundational step in managing your scholarly identity. It serves as a **hub** linking your outputs, affiliations, and professional activities.

> 💡 *ORCID is not just a profile—it’s your digital fingerprint in the research ecosystem.*

---

## RIS Profile (University of Nottingham)

At UoN, your **Research Information System (RIS)** profile extends the institutional representation of your research identity.  

RIS connects your outputs, projects, and impact activities with your ORCID iD to build a complete institutional record.

---

### Key Functions of RIS

- Displays **grants, outputs, and impact** in a central dashboard  
- Feeds information to your **public staff page** on the UoN website  
- Supports **REF reporting** and compliance requirements  
- Ensures consistent linking between institutional and external systems (e.g., funders, publishers, repositories)


## Other Profiles and Scholarly Platforms

Maintaining additional scholarly profiles enhances visibility across different discovery and citation systems.  

Each platform serves a slightly different purpose, and when synchronised with ORCID, they collectively form a **distributed network of your professional presence**.

---

| Platform | Purpose | Notes |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Scopus Author ID** | Tracks citation metrics, co-authorship, and affiliations | Automatically generated; may require merging duplicates |
| **Google Scholar** | Provides broad citation coverage and h-index | Useful for monitoring visibility; manual curation often required |
| **ResearchGate / Academia.edu** | Offer social networking layers for research exchange | Optional; use responsibly and avoid reliance on non-standard metrics |


> ⚠️ N.b. Always prioritise **validated platforms** (e.g., ORCID, Scopus, RIS) over social research sites that rely on proprietary or unverifiable metrics.

---

### Profile Management Tips

- **Set your ORCID profile to public** to maximise discoverability  
- **Merge duplicate Scopus IDs** to ensure accurate citation counts  
- **Link your ORCID iD with RIS** for automatic synchronisation of outputs  
- **Use consistent naming and affiliation formats** across all systems  
- **Avoid fabricated metrics** (e.g., RG Score) that lack transparency or scholarly validity  
- **Regularly audit** your profiles to ensure accuracy and consistency  

---

> 🌐 *Your digital identity is a living record of your research career. Managing it carefully ensures that your contributions are visible, citable, and connected across the scholarly network.*

---

## The Collaborative Dimension of Research Identity

A well-managed research identity does more than showcase your individual achievements—it **enables collaboration**.  

---

When researchers maintain accurate and connected profiles, others can:

- Identify relevant expertise for interdisciplinary projects  
- Attribute data and software contributions correctly  
- Track shared publications and outputs through persistent links  
- Reduce duplication and confusion in collaborative outputs  

---

In this sense, research identity is not only about self-presentation—it is an **infrastructure for collaboration, attribution, and trust** in the digital research ecosystem.

> “A connected identity enables connected research.”

---

::: callout-task 

#### Task 3: Research Identity Self-Audit

::: panel-tabset
##### Activity part 1

❓ Do you have a ORCID research identity? 

Visit [Orcid](https://orcid.org/), Log in / sign up

##### Activity part 2

❓ Is your ORCID iD is populated with your name, affiliation etc? if not make sure that is complete. 
- Set your ORCID ID to public (if appropriate)  

#####  Further Infomation

- You can actually link your RIS and ORCID profiles but for that you need to be registered to use worktribe. It is recomended that you [request access](https://nottingham-research.worktribe.com/login.jx)
- Once registered and logged in, you can link your ORCID and RIS profiles. For more infomation please refer to the [RIS training materials](http://moodle.nottingham.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=47898) to go away and read
:::
:::

# Digital Security & Good Practice

Effective research in the digital age requires more than technical competence, it demands **vigilance, responsibility, and sound data stewardship**.  

Digital security and good practice are not peripheral concerns; they are fundamental to protecting your research, collaborators, and institutional reputation.

---

Researchers handle sensitive data, intellectual property, and personal information. 

As such, maintaining **digital security hygiene** is a shared professional obligation.  

---

## Why Digital Security Matters in Research

- **Trust and compliance:** Funders, journals, and institutions require that research data be handled securely.  
- **Data integrity:** Protects against corruption, tampering, or unauthorised modification of research outputs.  
- **Confidentiality:** Prevents exposure of sensitive or identifiable information.  
- **Continuity:** Reduces the risk of data loss or research disruption caused by cyber incidents.  
- **Collaboration resilience:** Ensures that data shared across teams and institutions remains secure.

> 🔒 *Digital security is a collective responsibility—every researcher contributes to the safety and integrity of the wider research ecosystem.*

---

## Passwords & Access Management

Strong passwords are the first line of defence against unauthorised access. Weak or reused passwords remain one of the most common vulnerabilities in academic environments.

---

- Use passwords of **at least 12 characters**, combining uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols.  
- Create **unique passwords** for every institutional and external system you use.    
- Never share passwords or respond to credential requests via email, chat, or phone.  
- Regularly **review account permissions**—especially for collaborative tools—to ensure only current team members retain access.  

---

## Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

**MFA** adds a critical layer of protection by requiring an additional verification step beyond a password.  

It significantly reduces the risk of account compromise, even if your password is stolen.

> 🔐 *Think of MFA as the seatbelt of your digital life—simple, unobtrusive, and potentially life-saving.*

---

## Device & Data Protection

Your devices are gateways to your research data. Protecting them is essential for maintaining data integrity and institutional trust.

- Always store files on **UoN-approved cloud platforms** (OneDrive, SharePoint, Teams).  
- Avoid saving sensitive or active project files on **local drives** or **personal cloud services**.   
- Apply **automatic screen locks** and require a password or PIN for login.  
- When sharing devices temporarily (e.g. in labs or shared offices), use **separate user profiles** to maintain privacy and security.  

> ⚙️ *Encryption protects your data even if the device is lost or stolen.*

---

## Secure Remote Access

Remote work is now a standard part of research, but it introduces new security risks.  

- Use the **UoN VPN** when accessing institutional systems or sensitive datasets from outside the university network.  
- Avoid connecting through public Wi-Fi without a VPN—especially in airports, cafés, or hotels.  
- Keep your **operating system and software updated** before connecting remotely.  
- When working with collaborators in other institutions, clarify and document secure data access and sharing procedures.  

> 🌍 *A VPN creates a secure tunnel between your device and the university network, maintaining confidentiality and integrity of transmitted data.*

---

## Data Backup Strategy

Data loss can occur from accidental deletion, hardware failure, or ransomware.  
Implementing a structured backup policy is essential for ensuring continuity and recoverability.

- Follow the **3-2-1 Rule:**  
  - Keep **3 copies** of your data  
  - Store them on **2 different media types** (e.g., cloud + local external drive)  
  - Maintain **1 off-site or cloud-based backup**  
- Use **automated backups** (OneDrive and SharePoint offer versioning and recovery).  
- Document backup locations and frequencies as part of your project’s data management plan (DMP).  

> 💾 *A backup you have never tested is not a backup—it’s a hypothesis.*

---

## Cybersecurity Best Practices

Cybersecurity is not static—it requires constant awareness and proactive management.  

Every researcher should adopt the following baseline practices:

- **Verify sender identity** before opening attachments or clicking links.  
- **Avoid using personal email** for research-related communication or data exchange.  
- Report suspicious emails, data breaches, or security concerns **immediately** to UoN IT Security.  
- When collaborating internationally, ensure that shared data and platforms comply with relevant data protection laws (e.g. GDPR).  

> 🧩 *Cybersecurity is a daily habit, not a one-off task.*

## Towards a Culture of Digital Responsibility

Digital security is not only technical—it is cultural.  
A secure digital environment is built on **awareness, communication, and accountability**.  

By embedding these principles into daily workflows—strong passwords, MFA, secure storage, regular backups, and responsible sharing—you contribute to a **culture of trusted, reproducible, and secure research**.

> “Security and good practice underpin the credibility of research. Protecting data protects discovery.”


---

::: callout-task

#### Task 4: Digital Security Self-Audit (10 min)

::: panel-tabset
##### Question  
Reflect on your current digital setup. How secure is your research environment?

##### Exercise  
<details>
<summary><strong>Checklist</strong></summary>

- [ ] Passwords meet length and uniqueness guidelines  
- [ ] MFA is enabled (preferably with phone sign-in)  
- [ ] Files are stored in university-approved platforms  
- [ ] Device encryption is active  
- [ ] VPN used when working off-campus or with sensitive information  
</details>

##### Follow-up  
- Identify one action to improve your security in the coming week  
- Reflect on what was easy/hard to assess, and what barriers exist for better practice  
:::
:::

# Further Information

## UoN Training Resources

- Microsoft 365 Training Resources
- Excel 2016: Basic–Advanced (Online)
- Staff session: Managing your online and RIS research profiles
- Digital Accessibility: The Core Nottingham Accessible Practices
- RIS: Worktribe User Guidance 
- UniCore Training Materials
- UoN Information Security: Protect Your Data & Devices

---

::: callout-keypoints

## 📚 keypoints

- UoN currently uses Microsoft 365 tools support collaborative writing, file sharing, and communication
- UniCore manages research-related administration  and RIS manages the research lifecycle.
- A well-maintained research identity increases visibility and attribution of your work.
- Store research data in encrypted, university-backed platforms and ensure your account details are protected
:::

---

::: callout-hints

## 🔦 hints

- Store shared project files in Microsoft Teams rather than OneDrive to ensure long-term access for collaborators.
- Use version history in OneDrive/SharePoint instead of manually renaming file versions.
- Link your ORCID iD to your RIS profile to automate tracking of publications and outputs.
- Practice “security by default”
:::
